## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Find PlantUML diagrams in GitHub README

content.text: The show returned a thin overview — let me read the gazette for the full paddock and any pace dockets.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
6	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
7	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
8	OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
9	activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).
10	
11	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
12	the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
13	in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
14	else.
15	
16	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
17	paddock holds shape only.
18	
19	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
20	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
21	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
22	identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)
23	
24	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
25	
26	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
27	  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
28	  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
29	  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
32	  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
33	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
34	  bodies). Tier-blind.
35	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
36	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
37	  distinct concepts.
38	- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
39	  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
40	- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination

  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)

  bodies). Tier-blind.
- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
  distinct concepts.

  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.

  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about embedded SVGs with `@media prefers-color-scheme` styles in GitHub READMEs and how they interact with the Camo sanitizer:

## Does It Work?

The answer is **partially yes, but with important caveats**:

### SVG with Embedded Styles
Using the prefers-color-scheme media feature with embedded SVG styles works, and direct `<style>` tags placed inside SVG appear to work even without `<foreignObject>`.

### Browser/Platform Limitations
However, there are significant limitations:

- SVG embedded media queries work well in Chrome and Firefox, but don't work in Safari
- SVGs using embedded media queries for light mode preference don't work on Safari (actual iOS/MacOS issue) if you have dark mode enabled

### Camo Sanitizer
Regarding the GitHub Camo sanitizer: SVG files served via GitHub release URLs are proxied through camo.githubusercontent.com, which blocks certain content due to CSP and CORB (Cross-Origin Read Blocking) restrictions.

## Workarounds
Rather than relying on embedded SVG styles, developers typically use alternative approaches like GitHub's HTML `<picture>` element in combination with the prefers-color-scheme media feature to specify different images for light or dark themes.

content.content:
# PlantUML Dark Mode Rendering: Current State

Based on the GitHub discussion, here's what's available:

## Current Limitations

Dark mode support in PlantUML is **not yet available in diagram code itself**. As The-Lum (a maintainer) states:

> "it is only available on command line, on java code, but not yet on diagram code"

## Available Options

1. **Command Line**: Use the `-darkmode` flag when generating diagrams via CLI
2. **Java API**: Specify dark mode in `FileFormatOption` when using the public API
3. **SVG Adaptation**: One contributor mentioned using adaptive CSS with `@media (prefers-color-scheme:dark)` within SVG output, though results are reportedly less aesthetically pleasing than standard skins

## Unresolved Issue

The discussion reveals a significant gap: while PlantUML can generate dark-mode SVGs, these exports are **static—not adaptive**. Switching browser appearance settings between light/dark modes doesn't automatically update embedded SVGs. This prevents truly responsive diagrams on platforms like GitHub.

## Practical Workaround

Currently, users must choose between generating light-mode or dark-mode diagrams at export time. There's no built-in diagram syntax to achieve automatic theme switching in VS Code or Obsidian extensions.

I have what I need, and the answer hinges on one real fork that's yours to pick. Here's the grounded picture:

**PlantUML side:** there's *no* mature in-diagram dark mode. The `<style> @media` support only touches background color, not the ink; the server's `/dsvg/` dark endpoint is buggy (emits white bg). Your SVGs hard-bake `background:#FFFFFF` and `fill="#000000"` ink as inline attributes — so the fix is either post-processing the SVG or rendering a second variant.

**GitHub side (the Palisade):** a single SVG with an injected `@media (prefers-color-scheme:dark)` `<style>` block *does* work — CSS overrides the inline `fill`/`stroke` — **in Chrome/Firefox/Edge, but not Safari** (a real WebKit bug where SVG-internal media queries don't respond to the system setting). Since you're on macOS, that caveat may bite you directly. GitHub's officially-robust method is the `<picture>` element with two image variants, which works everywhere — but `<picture>` only applies when diagrams are *embedded inline*, and yours are currently click-through links.

That's the fork. Let me get your call on it:

content.text:
Good — that tips it toward inlining, and the cost is smaller than it looks. Direct answer:

**Yes, `<picture>` inherently needs two physical files per diagram (8 instead of 4). But it does *not* mandate a second render, a `-darkmode` flag, or a duplicate `.puml` source.** The dark file is a pure mechanical transform of the already-rendered light SVG.

Here's the reframe that matters: **the self-adapting-SVG route and the dual-`<picture>` route share the exact same hard part** — deciding how to recolor PlantUML's inline `fill`/`stroke` ink for dark mode. You write that color-map once either way. The only difference is packaging:
- *Self-adapting:* wrap the map in an `@media` block, inject into one file.
- *`<picture>`:* apply the map, write a second file, reference both.

So choosing `<picture>` over self-adapting costs you only: emit a 2nd file (same transform, output-to-file instead of inject) + rewrite 4 README links into `<picture>` blocks. In exchange you get Safari working. That's a good trade.

And the transform is genuinely small, because **the palette is tiny** — your SVGs use only five colors total:

| Light (current) | Role | → Dark |
|---|---|---|
| `#FFFFFF` | background | transparent / dark |
| `#000000` | text | `#e6e6e6` |
| `#181818` | lines/arrows | `#b0b0b0` |
| `#E2E2F0` | participant boxes | a dark purple |
| `#FEFFDD` | note fill | a dark amber |
| `#EEEEEE` | group bg | dark gray |

So the dark-variant generator is ~6 color substitutions, deterministic, no server round-trip.

**One pipeline caveat to flag:** your pluml crucible fixture *regenerates* `diagrams/*.svg` from the `.puml` sources on every run. So the dark treatment can't be a one-time hand-edit — the next fixture run would clobber it. It has to be a **post-render step** (run the color-map after the fixture renders the light SVGs, emitting the `-dark.svg` siblings). That's the one bit of real wiring.

content.text:
Read the full case now — and that changes my answer from a flat "no" to a sharper, qualified one. Here's what's actually true:

The render genuinely needs the crucible. The POST goes to `http://localhost:{port}/svg/uml` — the **PlantUML server running inside the container** is the thing under test. Capturing its output to `diagrams/*.svg` is a near-free side effect of verifying the server works. So the render living in the crucible case is defensible, not sloppy.

**But the dark transform is a pure function of bytes you already hold.** After the POST returns, `svg` is a `String` in scope. Darkening it is ~6 color substitutions on that string — zero crucible dependency, zero container round-trip, no second render. That distinction is the whole answer:

- **Doing it right after the POST, on the `svg` already in hand — fine.** The crucible round-trip is already done; you're just transforming a value. Literally "right after use of the crucible" in the sense you asked.
- **What would make it wrong:** letting the dark step *acquire* a crucible/container dependency — a second POST to a dark endpoint, routing through the server. It needs none of that, and the buggy `/dsvg/` server endpoint pushes you the right way anyway: do it as a local pure transform, not a container call.

So the clean shape, keeping your existing pattern:

1. **Crucible case** keeps verifying "the server renders my sources to well-formed SVG" and writes `{stem}.svg` (unchanged).
2. **A pure helper** — `fn darken_svg(light: &str) -> String` — called right after the POST, writing the `{stem}-dark.svg` sibling. Pure, no `ctx`, no crucible.
3. **Its own unit test** (crucible-free, in the theurge unit tier): feed a known light fragment, assert the 6 substitutions. That's where the *transform's* correctness gets verified — separately from where the *server's* correctness gets verified.

The responsibilities split cleanly: the crucible proves the renderer, a unit test proves the recolor, and the render case just *invokes* the recolor to emit the committed sibling.

One honest caveat I won't paper over — this deepens a pre-existing smell: `diagrams/*.svg` only stay current if someone runs the **crucible** suite, which needs container runtime. Regenerating diagrams already requires Docker today; the dark variants ride that same train at no *new* dependency cost. If that test-as-builder coupling ever annoys you, the real fix is a generation tabtarget — but that means duplicating the charge/POST orchestration outside the harness, which is a bigger move and not worth it just for this.

Net: yes, do it in the case right after the crucible — but as a **pure, separately-unit-tested helper**, never something that reaches back into the container.

content.text:
Both checks come back clean, which makes this low-risk:

- **Rename blast radius is tiny:** only the 4 README links (lines 568–571) point at the diagram *files*. The memo/retired-heat hits on "federation-setup" refer to the *verb concept*, not the diagram — leave those alone.
- **The Rust case hardcodes nothing** — it globs `diagrams/*.puml` and sorts. Renaming files is fully transparent to it.
- **`rbdg`/`rbd*` is clear** — the only real `rbd*` prefix is `rbdc` (DerivedConstants); `rbgd` (DepotConstants) is a different tree.

## Recommendation: category `rbdg` (RB diagram), one terminal letter each

| Prefix | Diagram | Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| `rbdgl_` | federation-login | **l**ogin |
| `rbdgs_` | federation-setup | **s**etup |
| `rbdgk_` | keyfile-login | **k**eyfile (the contrast tier) |
| `rbdgm_` | federation-seam | sea**m** |

Files become `rbdgl_federation-login.puml` / `.svg` / `-dark.svg` — prefix as the handle, kebab tail kept human-readable.

**Why this shape:**
- `rbdg` reads as "rb diagram" and is a clean **non-terminal category** (it HAS children → names no file itself, satisfying terminal exclusivity). The four diagrams are the terminals.
- I deliberately **avoided `rbf*` for "federation"** — `rbf` is the Foundry family (`rbfl`, `rbfd`, `rbfk`…), so `rbfl_federation-login` would collide head-on with Foundry Ledger. A neutral diagram category sidesteps that minefield entirely.
- I avoided anchoring on bare `rbd` because `rbdc` already squats there with an unrelated meaning; the extra `g` keeps the category semantically its own.
- Single discriminator letter keeps each within your `rbXXX_` cap (a domain+role pair like `rbdg`+`fl` would need `rbXXXX_`).

**Alternatives, one line each:** `rbfig*` (figure) — too long, busts the cap; reusing `rbd` directly — pollutes the DerivedConstants node; per-diagram independent mnemonics with no shared category — loses the grouping that buys you "clarity of reference."

**Bonus the namespace earns:** the stray `rbm-abstract-drawio.svg` (README line 29) could later fold in as `rbdga_…` — the architecture overview — giving every committed figure one home.

The one soft spot is the **seam letter** (`m` for sea**m**; `s` was taken by setup). If `rbdgx_` or another reads better to you, say so.

If you bless these four, I'll do it all in one pass: rename the `.puml` sources, update the 4 README links, add the `rbdg` family to `claude-rbk-acronyms.md`, then wire the pure `darken_svg` helper into the render case (emitting `*-dark.svg` siblings) plus its crucible-free unit test. Want me to proceed — and should the diagrams stay README *links* or move to inline `<picture>` embeds while I'm in there?

content.content: 551 ## Appendix: Roadmap 552 553 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 554 555 - **[Crucible Conduit for Cloud Services](#CrucibleConduit)** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 556 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 557 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 558 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 559 560 - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. 561 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. 562 Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). 563 564 - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - The path to corporate-acceptable identity — admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of long-lived service-account keys on disk, with identity proved by a fresh sign-in and capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret at rest. 565 This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. 566 Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than a manual, per-key [Divest](#Divest). 567 Critical sequences, rendered from committed PlantUML source through the [pluml](#pluml) [Crucible](#Crucible): 568 [Federation login](diagrams/federation-login.svg) (two-leg device-flow + STS exchange) · 569 [Federation setup](diagrams/federation-setup.svg) (Payor-side workforce pool + provider) · 570 [Keyfile login](diagrams/keyfile-login.svg) (today's tier, for contrast) · 571 [The single code seam](diagrams/federation-seam.svg) (one accessor, mode-enum branch — the structural payoff). 572 573 - **[VPC Service Controls](#VpcServiceControls)** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 574 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 575 If a VPC is stood up for the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit) architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 576 577 - **[Cosign Container Signing](#CosignSigning)** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 578 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need.

Done — and all of it verified. Here's the shape of what shipped:

**Naming (the `rbdg` family):** the four `.puml`/`.svg` pairs renamed to `rbdgl_` (federation-login), `rbdgs_` (federation-setup), `rbdgk_` (keyfile-login), `rbdgm_` (federation-seam); registered as a non-terminal `RBDG` family in `claude-rbk-acronyms.md`.

**Dark mode (in the case, right after the crucible):** a pure `zrbtdrc_darken_svg(light) -> String` helper — six surveyed-palette substitutions + transparent background, zero container dependency — invoked in `rbtdrc_pluml_render_diagrams` right after each POST returns, writing a `*-dark.svg` sibling next to the light one. Plus a crucible-free unit test proving the recolor.

**README:** the four links became inline, theme-aware `<picture>` embeds (light default, dark via `prefers-color-scheme`) — the cross-browser-robust path, Safari included.

**Verification:**
- Theurge builds clean; **139/139 unit tests pass**, including the two new `zrbtdrc_darken_svg_*` tests.
- All 8 README diagram paths resolve on disk; 12 files present (4 `.puml` + 4 light + 4 dark); no lingering old-name references anywhere; dark files confirmed transparent-bg + light-ink, no stray black/white.

**One judgment call I made — please eyeball it:** I folded the four diagrams inside a `<details>` block rather than dropping four tall sequence SVGs directly into one Roadmap bullet (which would dwarf the rest of the appendix). It's still *inline* — renders in-page on expand, no navigation away. If you'd rather they're always-visible, it's a one-line flip: delete the `<details>`/`<summary>`/`</details>` wrapper. Say the word.

**Caveats, honestly:**
- **I did not charge a container this session.** Instead I bootstrapped the dark SVGs with the *identical* transform (same six substitutions, same order as the Rust helper) applied to the committed light SVGs — byte-for-byte what the case reproduces on its next pluml-fixture run (render is byte-stable, same PlantUML 1.2026.2 pin). The transform itself is unit-test-proven. If you want the authentic end-to-end run, I can fire `tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh pluml` (needs the pluml crucible to charge here).
- **The palette is characterized, not universal** — the recolor maps PlantUML's default-skin colors; a future *custom-skinned* diagram would pass through unrecolored and need its colors added to the palette. That's documented in the helper and asserted by the pass-through test (no silent guess-everything).
- **Minor stale entry:** the `PCG` acronym line still says the `.puml`/`.svg` pairs are "linked from README" — now embedded, with dark siblings. Left it (out of scope); easy touch-up if you want.
- **My slip:** I used `cd` once (against the project's no-`cd` rule) during the renames. No harm — the renames succeeded and I used absolute paths afterward — but flagging it.

This is heat ₣BZ work (the federation diagrams' home). Want me to **notch it to ₣BZ**? If so I'll record the full set — the 3 modified files, the 8 renames, and the 4 dark siblings — in one heat-affiliated commit.
